verses.text: Remember the way the LORD led thee these forty years in the wilderness — to humble thee, to know what was in thine heart. He suffered thee to hunger, then fed thee with manna; that man doth not live by bread alone, but by every word out of the mouth of the LORD. Thy raiment waxed not old, thy foot swelled not, these forty years. As a man chasteneth his son, so the LORD chasteneth thee. Guidance: Forty years to teach one lesson: the bread that keeps you alive comes from God's mouth, not the bakery. He hungered them to feed them, that they might know the source. Even the discipline is fathering — not punishment for its own sake.

Keep the commandments, walk in His ways, fear Him. For the LORD bringeth thee into a good land — a land of brooks of water, fountains; wheat, barley, vines, fig trees, pomegranates; oil olive and honey; a land wherein thou shalt eat bread without scarceness, lack nothing; whose stones are iron, out of whose hills thou mayest dig brass. Guidance: Eight items in the list — five plants, two metals, and water under everything. God's good land is not vague paradise; it is a real country with measurable resources. The promise has crops you can name.

When thou hast eaten and art full, then thou shalt bless the LORD thy God for the good land which He hath given thee. Guidance: Grace after meals — Jewish tradition built this verse into every table. The fullness is not the achievement; the giver is. Eight words to say at the end of dinner: thank You, this came from You. The hardest place to remember God is the easy one.

Beware that thou forget not the LORD thy God: lest when thou hast eaten and art full, hast built goodly houses, herds and flocks multiplied, silver and gold multiplied — then thine heart be lifted up, and thou forget the LORD thy God which brought thee forth out of Egypt, from the house of bondage. Guidance: The forgetfulness arrives quietly, with the silverware. Not in the desert, not in the hunger, not in the war — but in the well-stocked kitchen. Prosperity is the harder test. Lifted hearts forget the lifter.

Who led thee through that great wilderness — fiery serpents, scorpions, drought; who brought thee water out of the rock of flint; who fed thee with manna which thy fathers knew not, that He might humble thee, prove thee, to do thee good at thy latter end. Guidance: The wilderness was not a detour. It was God doing thee good at thy latter end. Every snake survived, every dry day, every miracle on a rock — all sowing seeds whose harvest is years away. He is never in a hurry; He is always in the long good.

Lest thou say in thine heart: my power and the might of mine hand hath gotten me this wealth. But thou shalt remember the LORD thy God: for it is He that giveth thee power to get wealth, that He may establish His covenant which He sware unto thy fathers, as it is this day. Guidance: Even the strength to swing a hammer is on loan. The wealth is yours to handle; the hand is His to grant. He gives power to get wealth — not so wealth would be the goal, but so the covenant would be kept. Money is a tool of promise-keeping in God's economy.

If thou do at all forget the LORD thy God, walk after other gods, serve and worship them, I testify against you this day that ye shall surely perish. As the nations which the LORD destroyeth before your face, so shall ye perish; because ye would not be obedient unto the voice of the LORD your God. Guidance: Forgetting is the precursor; idolatry is the result. The chapter began with a warning and ends with a sentence. The same God who slew the nations to give Israel the land will not spare Israel for repeating their sins. Adoption is real; impunity is not.
